result:
`adr-001.md` is written. Here's the shape of what I delivered and the reasoning behind it.

## The core move

The hardest thing in your write-up isn't any single constraint — it's that the EU deadline tempts you to drag the microservices question and the APAC fix along with it, under a clock, with a team that can't absorb that. So the ADR's spine is a **reframe**: *residency is a data-locality and tenant-routing problem, not a decomposition problem.* Once you separate those, the three forces get put on their own clocks instead of colliding.

## What I committed to

**Regional cells of the same monolith** — same codebase deployed per region, each tenant pinned to a home region, *all* of that tenant's data (config + PII) living in its cell, with a thin global directory for routing. The decisive advantage over the "minimal patch" is that it **sidesteps the PII-column-tagging project nobody scoped** — whole-tenant locality means you never argue column-by-column with legal — and it generalizes to the 15%-and-rising EU trend instead of patching one customer.

Sequencing, explicitly: EU cell first (the only fixed clock) → APAC via collapsing the sequential booking-page round-trips *before* building a region (the 7-month renewal gives runway) → microservices declined now, gated behind triggers.

## Honest costs I didn't soften

The routing layer/global directory is a new critical-path SPOF; you're now running N cells with 6 frozen people (per-cell migrations, drift, more on-call); and cross-region analytics breaks your single-read-replica model. Those are in Consequences as real ongoing taxes, not footnotes.

## Assumptions I tagged and moved past
- Residency handled at **tenant** level, not end-customer level (routing depends on this).
- Budget ~+$5–8k/month, inside your "double" envelope.

Two things worth your eye before it goes to the CEO: whether legal will accept **tenant-level** residency granularity (the whole routing model rests on it), and whether you're comfortable telling the two pro-services engineers "not now, here's the trigger" — the ADR makes that an explicit, defensible position rather than a brush-off, but it is a people decision as much as a technical one.
